text.NRSV: Know well the condition of your flocks, and give attention to your herds;
text.Rotherham: Note well, the appearance of thy flock, apply thy mind to thy herds;

text.NRSV: for riches do not last forever, nor a crown for all generations.
text.Rotherham: For, not age-abiding, are riches, nor is the diadem, from generation to generation:—

text.NRSV: When the grass is gone, and new growth appears, and the herbage of the mountains is gathered,
text.Rotherham: The grass, is taken away, and the young shoot, showeth itself, and the herbage of the mountains, is gathered;

text.NRSV: the lambs will provide your clothing, and the goats the price of a field;
text.Rotherham: There are, lambs, for thy clothing, and, for the price of thy field, there are he-goats;

text.NRSV: there will be enough goats’ milk for your food, for the food of your household and maintenance for your servant-girls.
text.Rotherham: With, enough goats-milk, for thy food—for the food of thy household, and, a maintenance, for thy maidens.

text.NRSV: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
text.Rotherham: The lawless, fleeth when no man pursueth, but, the righteous, like a lion, are confident.

text.NRSV: When a land rebels, it has many rulers; but with an intelligent ruler there is lasting order.
text.Rotherham: For the transgressions of a land, many are the rulers thereof, but, under an intelligent and discerning man, stability is prolonged.

text.NRSV: A ruler who oppresses the poor is a beating rain that leaves no food.
text.Rotherham: A poor man, who oppresseth the helpless, [is like] a rain beating down, leaving no food.

text.NRSV: Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law struggle against them.
text.Rotherham: They who forsake instruction, praise one who is lawless, while, they who keep instruction, are at strife with them.

text.NRSV: The evil do not understand justice, but those who seek the Lord understand it completely.
text.Rotherham: Wicked men, consider not justice, but, they who seek Yahweh, consider everything.

text.NRSV: Better to be poor and walk in integrity than to be crooked in one’s ways even though rich.
text.Rotherham: Better a poor man walking in his integrity, than one who is crooked—turning two ways, though, he, be rich.

text.NRSV: Those who keep the law are wise children, but companions of gluttons shame their parents.
text.Rotherham: He that keepeth instruction, is a son with discernment, but, a companion of squanderers, bringeth shame to his father.

text.NRSV: One who augments wealth by exorbitant interest gathers it for another who is kind to the poor.
text.Rotherham: He that increaseth his substance by interest and profit, for one ready to favour the poor, doth gather it.

text.NRSV: When one will not listen to the law, even one’s prayers are an abomination.
text.Rotherham: He that turneth away his ear from hearing instruction, even his prayer, is an abomination.

text.NRSV: Those who mislead the upright into evil ways will fall into pits of their own making, but the blameless will have a goodly inheritance.
text.Rotherham: He that misguideth the upright into a hurtful way! into his own pit, he himself, shall fall, but, men of integrity, shall inherit good.

text.NRSV: The rich is wise in self-esteem, but an intelligent poor person sees through the poses.
text.Rotherham: Wise in his own eyes, is the man that is rich, but, a poor man of discernment, searcheth him out.

text.NRSV: When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, people hide themselves.
text.Rotherham: In the exulting of the righteous, there is great glorying, but, when the lawless arise, a man must be sought for.

text.NRSV: No one who conceals transgressions will prosper, but one who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy.
text.Rotherham: He that covereth his transgressions, shall not prosper, but, he that confesseth and forsaketh, shall find compassion.

text.NRSV: Happy is the one who fears the Lord always, but the stubborn of heart will fall into calamity.
text.Rotherham: How happy the man who is ever circumspect, whereas, he that hardeneth his heart, shall fall into calamity.

text.NRSV: Like a roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
text.Rotherham: A growling lion, and a ranging bear, is a lawless ruler, over a poor people.

text.NRSV: A ruler who lacks understanding is a cruel oppressor; but one who hates unjust gain will enjoy a long life.
text.Rotherham: A leader, may lack intelligence, yet abound in oppressions, The hater of greed, shall lengthen out days.

text.NRSV: If someone is burdened with the blood of another, let that person be a fugitive until death; let no one help.
text.Rotherham: A man oppressed with a person’s blood, unto a pit, shall flee, let them not hold him back.

text.NRSV: One who walks in integrity will be safe, but whoever follows crooked ways will fall into the Pit.
text.Rotherham: He that walketh with integrity, shall be saved, but, he that is crooked, turning two ways, shall fall in one.

text.NRSV: Anyone who tills the land will have plenty of bread, but one who follows worthless pursuits will have plenty of poverty.
text.Rotherham: He that tilleth his ground, shall have plenty of bread, but, he that pursueth empty-heads, shall have plenty of poverty.

text.NRSV: The faithful will abound with blessings, but one who is in a hurry to be rich will not go unpunished.
text.Rotherham: A man of fidelity, aboundeth in blessings, but, one hasting to be rich, shall not be held innocent.

text.NRSV: To show partiality is not good— yet for a piece of bread a person may do wrong.
text.Rotherham: To take note of faces [in judgment], is not good, and, for a bit of bread, a man will transgress.

text.NRSV: The miser is in a hurry to get rich and does not know that loss is sure to come.
text.Rotherham: A man, hasting to be rich, hath an evil eye, and knoweth not when want may overtake him.

text.NRSV: Whoever rebukes another will afterward find more favor than one who flatters with the tongue.
text.Rotherham: He that reproveth a man, shall, afterwards, find more, favour, than he that useth a flattering tongue.

text.NRSV: Anyone who robs father or mother and says, “That is no transgression,” is a partner to one who destroys.
text.Rotherham: He that robbeth his father or his mother, and saith, It is no transgression, companion, is he to one who wasteth.

text.NRSV: The greedy person stirs up strife, but whoever trusts in the Lord will be enriched.
text.Rotherham: The ambitious in soul, stirreth up strife, but, he that trusteth in Yahweh, shall be enriched.

text.NRSV: Those who trust in their own wits are fools, but those who walk in wisdom come through safely.
text.Rotherham: He that trusteth his own heart, the same, is a dullard, but, he that walketh wisely, the same, shall be delivered.

text.NRSV: Whoever gives to the poor will lack nothing, but one who turns a blind eye will get many a curse.
text.Rotherham: One who giveth to the poor, shall have no want, but, he that hideth his eyes, shall receive many a curse.

text.NRSV: When the wicked rise, people hide themselves, but when they perish, the righteous increase.
text.Rotherham: When lawless men rise, a common man will hide himself, but, when they perish, righteous men multiply.

text.NRSV: One who is often reproved, yet remains stiff-necked, will suddenly be broken beyond repair.
text.Rotherham: He that being often reproved stiffeneth his neck, suddenly shall be hurt, and there be no healing.

text.NRSV: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice; but when the wicked rule, the people groan.
text.Rotherham: When the righteous become great, the people rejoice, but, when a lawless man beareth rule, a people sigh.

text.NRSV: A child who loves wisdom makes a father glad, but to keep company with prostitutes is to squander one’s substance.
text.Rotherham: A man who loveth wisdom, gladdeneth his father, but, a companion of harlots, destroyeth wealth.

text.NRSV: By justice a king gives stability to the land, but one who makes heavy exactions ruins it.
text.Rotherham: A king, by justice, shall establish a land,—but, a man open to bribes, bringeth it to ruin.

text.NRSV: Whoever flatters a neighbor is spreading a net for the neighbor’s feet.
text.Rotherham: A man who flattereth his neighbour, spreadeth, a net, over his steps.

text.NRSV: In the transgression of the evil there is a snare, but the righteous sing and rejoice.
text.Rotherham: In the transgression of a wicked man, is a snare, but, the righteous, doth shout in triumph and rejoice.

text.NRSV: The righteous know the rights of the poor; the wicked have no such understanding.
text.Rotherham: The righteous doth acknowledge the plea of the poor, but, the lawless, regardeth not knowledge.

text.NRSV: Scoffers set a city aflame, but the wise turn away wrath.
text.Rotherham: Men given to mockery, inflame a city,—but, wise men, turn away anger.

text.NRSV: If the wise go to court with a fool, there is only fretting and laughing, and no rest.
text.Rotherham: A wise man pleading with a foolish man, whether he rage or laugh, there is no settlement.

text.NRSV: The bloodthirsty hate the blameless, and they seek the life of the upright.
text.Rotherham: Blood-thirsty men, hate the blameless man, and, as for the upright, they seek his life.

text.NRSV: A fool gives full vent to anger, but the wise quietly holds it back.
text.Rotherham: All his anger, doth a dullard let go, but, a wise man, by keeping it back, stilleth it.

text.NRSV: If a ruler listens to falsehood, all his officials will be wicked.
text.Rotherham: When a ruler giveth heed to the word of falsehood, all his attendants, become lawless.

text.NRSV: The poor and the oppressor have this in common: the Lord gives light to the eyes of both.
text.Rotherham: The poor man and the man of usury, meet together, he that enlighteneth the eyes of them both, is Yahweh.

text.NRSV: If a king judges the poor with equity, his throne will be established forever.
text.Rotherham: When a king judgeth faithfully the poor, his throne, to futurity, shall be established.

text.NRSV: The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a mother is disgraced by a neglected child.
text.Rotherham: A rod with rebuke, giveth wisdom, but, a youth unrestrained, bringeth shame to his mother.

text.NRSV: When the wicked are in authority, transgression increases, but the righteous will look upon their downfall.
text.Rotherham: When the lawless become great, transgression increaseth, but, the righteous, shall behold, their ruin.

text.NRSV: Discipline your children, and they will give you rest; they will give delight to your heart.
text.Rotherham: Correct thy son, that he may give thee rest, that he may give delight to thy soul.

text.NRSV: Where there is no prophecy, the people cast off restraint, but happy are those who keep the law.
text.Rotherham: Where there is no vision, a people is let loose, but, he that keepeth instruction, how happy is he!

text.NRSV: By mere words servants are not disciplined, for though they understand, they will not give heed.
text.Rotherham: By words, a servant will not be corrected, though he perceiveth, yet is there no answer.

text.NRSV: Do you see someone who is hasty in speaking? There is more hope for a fool than for such a person.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast seen a man hasty in his words,—there is, more hope of a dullard, than of him.

text.NRSV: A slave pampered from childhood will become a rebel in the end.
text.Rotherham: He that dealeth tenderly with his servant from childhood, in his after life, shall have him for a son.

text.NRSV: One given to anger stirs up strife, and the hot-tempered person causes many a transgression.
text.Rotherham: A man given to anger, stirreth up strife, and, he that exceedeth in wrath, aboundeth in transgression.

text.NRSV: A person’s pride will bring humiliation, but one who is lowly in spirit will obtain honor.
text.Rotherham: The loftiness of a man, layeth him low,—but, one of a lowly spirit, shall attain unto honour.

text.NRSV: To be a partner of a thief is to hate one’s own life; one hears the victim’s curse, but discloses nothing.
text.Rotherham: He that shareth with a thief, hateth himself, an oath, he heareth, yet may not tell.

text.NRSV: The fear of others lays a snare, but one who trusts in the Lord is secure.
text.Rotherham: The fear of man, setteth a snare, but, he that trusteth in Yahweh, shall be placed on high.

text.NRSV: Many seek the favor of a ruler, but it is from the Lord that one gets justice.
text.Rotherham: Many, seek the face of a ruler, but, from Yahweh, is the sentence of each one.

text.NRSV: The unjust are an abomination to the righteous, and those whose way is straight are an abomination to the wicked.
text.Rotherham: An abomination to the righteous, is the man of perversity, and, an abomination to the lawless, is a man of straightforward way.

text.NRSV: The words of Agur son of Jakeh. An oracle. Thus says the man: I am weary, O God, I am weary, O God. How can I prevail?
text.Rotherham: The words of Agur, son of Jakeh, [even] the oracle,—The utterance of the man, for Ithiel, for Ithiel and Ucal.

text.NRSV: Surely I am too stupid to be human; I do not have human understanding.
text.Rotherham: Surely, more brutish, am, I, than any man, nor doth, the understanding of a son of earth, pertain to me;

text.NRSV: I have not learned wisdom, nor have I knowledge of the holy ones.
text.Rotherham: Neither have I learned wisdom, nor, the knowledge of the Holy Ones, can I acquire.

text.NRSV: Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in the hollow of the hand? Who has wrapped up the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is the name, and what is the name of the child? Surely you know!
text.Rotherham: Who hath ascended the heavens and then descended? Who hath gathered the wind into his two hands? Who hath wrapped up the waters in a mantle? Who hath set up all the ends of the earth? What is his name and what the name of his son, when thou knowest?

text.NRSV: Every word of God proves true; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.
text.Rotherham: Every saying of God is refined, A shield, is, he, to them who seek refuge in him.

text.NRSV: Do not add to his words, or he will rebuke you, and you will be found a liar.
text.Rotherham: Do not add unto his words, lest he convict thee, and thou be found false.

text.NRSV: Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die:
text.Rotherham: Two things, have I asked of thee, withhold them not from me, ere yet I die:

text.NRSV: Remove far from me falsehood and lying; give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with the food that I need,
text.Rotherham: Vanity and falsehood, remove far from me, Neither poverty nor riches, give me, Feed me with the food appointed me:

text.NRSV: or I shall be full, and deny you, and say, “Who is the Lord?” or I shall be poor, and steal, and profane the name of my God.
text.Rotherham: Lest I be full, and deny, and say—Who is Yahweh? or lest I be impoverished and steal, and do violence to the Name of my God.

text.NRSV: Do not slander a servant to a master, or the servant will curse you, and you will be held guilty.
text.Rotherham: Do not slander a servant to his master, lest he revile thee, and thou be found guilty.

text.NRSV: There are those who curse their fathers and do not bless their mothers.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Its father, it revileth, and, its mother, it doth not bless.

text.NRSV: There are those who are pure in their own eyes yet are not cleansed of their filthiness.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Pure in its own eyes, yet, from its filth, hath it not been bathed,

text.NRSV: There are those—how lofty are their eyes, how high their eyelids lift!
text.Rotherham: A generation! How lofty are its eyes, and its eyelashes uplifted.

text.NRSV: There are those whose teeth are swords, whose teeth are knives, to devour the poor from off the earth, the needy from among mortals.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Swords, are its teeth, and, knives, its incisors,—to devour the humbled out of the earth, and the needy, from among men.

text.NRSV: The leech has two daughters; “Give, give,” they cry. Three things are never satisfied; four never say, “Enough”:
text.Rotherham: The vampire, hath two daughters, Give! Give! Three, there are will not be satisfied, four, have not said, Enough!

text.NRSV: Sheol, the barren womb, the earth ever thirsty for water, and the fire that never says, “Enough.”
text.Rotherham: Hades, and barrenness,—A land not satisfied with water, and fire, that saith not, Enough!

text.NRSV: The eye that mocks a father and scorns to obey a mother will be pecked out by the ravens of the valley and eaten by the vultures.
text.Rotherham: The eye that mocketh a father, and despiseth to obey a mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out, and the young of the eagle shall eat it.

text.NRSV: Three things are too wonderful for me; four I do not understand:
text.Rotherham: Three, things there are, too difficult for me, yea, four, which I do not understand:

text.NRSV: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent on a rock, the way of a ship on the high seas, and the way of a man with a girl.
text.Rotherham: The way of an eagle in the heavens, the way of a serpent on the rock,—the way of a ship in the heart of the sea, and the way of a man with a maid.

text.NRSV: This is the way of an adulteress: she eats, and wipes her mouth, and says, “I have done no wrong.”
text.Rotherham: So, is the way of a woman committing adultery,—she eateth, and wipeth her mouth, and saith, I have done no iniquity!

text.NRSV: Under three things the earth trembles; under four it cannot bear up:
text.Rotherham: Under three things, a land is stirred, yea, under four, she cannot bear up:

text.NRSV: a slave when he becomes king, and a fool when glutted with food;
text.Rotherham: Under a servant, when he reigneth, and a base man, when he is surfeited with food;

text.NRSV: an unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maid when she succeeds her mistress.
text.Rotherham: Under a hateful woman, when she is married, and a handmaid when she driveth out her mistress.

text.NRSV: Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly wise:
text.Rotherham: Four, things there are, the small of the earth,—yet, they, are wiser than the wise:

text.NRSV: the ants are a people without strength, yet they provide their food in the summer;
text.Rotherham: The ants, a people, not strong,—yet prepare they, in summer, their food;

text.NRSV: the badgers are a people without power, yet they make their homes in the rocks;
text.Rotherham: The conies, a people of, no power, yet set they, among the crags, their house;

text.NRSV: the locusts have no king, yet all of them march in rank;
text.Rotherham: King, is there none, to, the locusts,—yet go forth in swarms, do they all;

text.NRSV: the lizard can be grasped in the hand, yet it is found in kings’ palaces.
text.Rotherham: The lizard, with hands, reneweth its hold,—yet, the same, is in the palaces of the king.

text.NRSV: Three things are stately in their stride; four are stately in their gait:
text.Rotherham: Three, things there are which step along well, ye, four, which excel in going:

text.NRSV: the lion, which is mightiest among wild animals and does not turn back before any;
text.Rotherham: The lion, hero among beasts, which turneth aside from the face of no one;

text.NRSV: the strutting cock, the he-goat, and a king striding before his people.
text.Rotherham: The greyhound, or the he-goat,—and a king, having a band of soldiers with him.

text.NRSV: If you have been foolish, exalting yourself, or if you have been devising evil, put your hand on your mouth.
text.Rotherham: If thou hast acted basely by lifting thyself up,—if thou hast plotted evil, [put thy] hand to [thy] mouth!

text.NRSV: For as churning milk produces butter, and twisting the nose produces blood, so forcing anger produces strife.
text.Rotherham: Surely, the pressing of milk, bringeth forth curd, and, the pressing of the nose, bringeth forth blood, and, the pressing of wrath, bringeth forth strife.

text.NRSV: The words of King Lemuel. An oracle that his mother taught him:
text.Rotherham: The words of Lemuel the king,—the strain which was taught him by his mother:—

text.NRSV: No, my son! No, son of my womb! No, son of my vows!
text.Rotherham: What, my son? and what, the son of my womb? aye what, the son of my vows?

text.NRSV: Do not give your strength to women, your ways to those who destroy kings.
text.Rotherham: Do not give, to women, thy strength, nor thy ways, to them who ruin kings.

text.NRSV: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to desire strong drink;
text.Rotherham: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, nor, for dignitaries, to desire strong drink,

text.NRSV: or else they will drink and forget what has been decreed, and will pervert the rights of all the afflicted.
text.Rotherham: Lest he drink, and forget that which is decreed, and alter the plea of any who are sorely oppressed.

text.NRSV: Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to those in bitter distress;
text.Rotherham: Give strong drink, to him that is perishing, and wine, to such as are embittered in soul:

text.NRSV: let them drink and forget their poverty, and remember their misery no more.
text.Rotherham: Let him drink, and forget his poverty, and, his wearying toil, let him remember no more.

text.NRSV: Speak out for those who cannot speak, for the rights of all the destitute.
text.Rotherham: Open thy mouth for the dumb, for the cause of all the children of the departed.

text.NRSV: Speak out, judge righteously, defend the rights of the poor and needy.
text.Rotherham: Open thy mouth—judge righteously,—and administer justice for the poor and the needy.

text.NRSV: A capable wife who can find? She is far more precious than jewels.
text.Rotherham: A virtuous woman, who can find? for, far beyond corals, is her worth.

text.NRSV: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he will have no lack of gain.
text.Rotherham: The heart of her husband, trusteth her, and, gain, he shall not lack:

text.NRSV: She does him good, and not harm, all the days of her life.
text.Rotherham: She doeth him good and not evil, all the days of her life:

text.NRSV: She seeks wool and flax, and works with willing hands.
text.Rotherham: She seeketh wool and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands:

text.NRSV: She is like the ships of the merchant, she brings her food from far away.
text.Rotherham: She is like the ships of the merchant, from afar, she bringeth in her food;

text.NRSV: She rises while it is still night and provides food for her household and tasks for her servant-girls.
text.Rotherham: And she riseth, while yet it is night, and giveth food to her household, and a task to her maidens:

text.NRSV: She considers a field and buys it; with the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.
text.Rotherham: She considereth a field, and procureth it, Out of the fruit of her hands, she planteth a vineyard:

text.NRSV: She girds herself with strength, and makes her arms strong.
text.Rotherham: She girdeth, with strength her loins, and putteth vigour into her arms:

text.NRSV: She perceives that her merchandise is profitable. Her lamp does not go out at night.
text.Rotherham: She tasteth, whether, good, be her merchandise, and her lamp, goeth not out by night:

text.NRSV: She puts her hands to the distaff, and her hands hold the spindle.
text.Rotherham: Her hands, she putteth forth to the distaff, and, her palms, lay hold of the spindle:

text.NRSV: She opens her hand to the poor, and reaches out her hands to the needy.
text.Rotherham: Her palm, she spreadeth out to the oppressed, and, her hands, she extendeth to the needy:

text.NRSV: She is not afraid for her household when it snows, for all her household are clothed in crimson.
text.Rotherham: She feareth not, for her household, because of the snow, for, all her household, are clothed with crimson:

text.NRSV: She makes herself coverings; her clothing is fine linen and purple.
text.Rotherham: Coverlets, she maketh for herself, Of white linen and of purple, is her clothing:

text.NRSV: Her husband is known in the city gates, taking his seat among the elders of the land.
text.Rotherham: Known in the gates, is her husband, when he sitteth, with the elders of the land:

text.NRSV: She makes linen garments and sells them; she supplies the merchant with sashes.
text.Rotherham: Fine linen wraps, she maketh and selleth, and, girdles, doth she deliver to the trader:

text.NRSV: Strength and dignity are her clothing, and she laughs at the time to come.
text.Rotherham: Strength and dignity, are her clothing, and she laugheth at the time to come:

text.NRSV: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.
text.Rotherham: Her mouth, she openeth with wisdom, and, the instruction of kindness, is on her tongue:

text.NRSV: She looks well to the ways of her household, and does not eat the bread of idleness.
text.Rotherham: She looketh well to the goings of her household, and, the bread of idleness, will she not eat.

text.NRSV: Her children rise up and call her happy; her husband also, and he praises her:
text.Rotherham: Her children rise up, and call her happy! her husband, and he praiseth her:—

text.NRSV: “Many women have done excellently, but you surpass them all.”
text.Rotherham: Many daughters, have done virtuously, but, thou, excellest them all!

text.NRSV: Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vain, but a woman who fears the Lord is to be praised.
text.Rotherham: Deceitful is loveliness and vain is beauty, The woman that revereth Yahweh, she, shall be praised:

text.NRSV: Give her a share in the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the city gates.
text.Rotherham: Give her of the fruit of her own hands, and let her own works, praise her in the gates.
